content:
# Luke Chapter 15 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 107-108 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_015_page_001.jpg, LUK_015_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Now all the tax collectors and sinners kept coming to listen to Jesus. **2** And the Pharisees and the scribes were grumbling, saying, "This man welcomes sinners and eats with them." **3** So Jesus told them this parable:

**4** "Suppose one of you has a hundred sheep and loses one of them. Does he not leave the ninety-nine in the open country and go after the lost one until he finds it? **5** And when he finds it, he joyfully puts it on his shoulders **6** and goes home. Then he calls his friends and neighbors together and says, 'Celebrate with me! I have found my lost sheep!' **7** I tell you, in the same way there will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous people who do not need to repent.

**8** "Or suppose a woman has ten silver coins and loses one of them. Does she not light a lamp, sweep the house, and search carefully until she finds it? **9** And when she finds it, she calls her friends and neighbors together and says, 'Celebrate with me! I have found the coin I lost!' **10** In the same way, I tell you, there is joy in the presence of God's angels over one sinner who repents."

**11** Then Jesus said, "A man had two sons. **12** The younger one said to his father, 'Father, give me my share of the estate.' So the father divided his property between them. **13** Not many days later, the younger son gathered everything he had and left for a distant country, where he squandered his wealth in reckless living. **14** After he had spent everything, a severe famine struck that whole country, and he began to be in need. **15** So he went and hired himself out to one of the citizens of that country, who sent him into his fields to feed pigs. **16** He longed to fill his stomach with the pods the pigs were eating, but no one gave him anything.

**17** "When he came to his senses, he said, 'How many of my father's hired workers have more than enough food, and here I am, starving to death! **18** I will get up and go back to my father and say to him: Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you. **19** I am no longer worthy to be called your son. Treat me as one of your hired workers.' **20** So he got up and went to his father.

"But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and was filled with compassion for him. He ran to his son, threw his arms around him, and kissed him. **21** The son said to him, 'Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son.'

**22** "But the father said to his servants, 'Quick! Bring out the best robe and put it on him. Put a ring on his finger and sandals on his feet. **23** Bring the fattened calf and kill it. Let us eat and celebrate! **24** For this son of mine was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found!' And they began to celebrate.

**25** "Meanwhile, the older son was out in the field. As he came near the house, he heard music and dancing. **26** So he called one of the servants and asked what was going on. **27** The servant told him, 'Your brother has come home, and your father has killed the fattened calf because he has him back safe and sound.'

**28** "The older brother became angry and refused to go in. So his father went out and pleaded with him. **29** But he answered his father, 'Look! All these years I have slaved for you and never disobeyed a single one of your commands. Yet you never gave me even a young goat so I could celebrate with my friends. **30** But when this son of yours comes back — the one who devoured your property with prostitutes — you kill the fattened calf for him!'

**31** "'My child,' the father said, 'you are always with me, and everything I have is yours. **32** But we had to celebrate and be glad, because this brother of yours was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found.'"

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

- **15:1-2 — "This man welcomes sinners and eats with them"**: The Pharisees and scribes intend this as an accusation, but Luke has arranged it as the thesis statement for one of the most important chapters in all of Scripture. The verb PROSDECHETAI (prosdechetai, "welcomes" or "receives") implies not merely tolerating the presence of sinners but actively extending hospitality to them. And SYNESTHIEI (synesthiei, "eats with") is the truly scandalous charge. In the ancient Jewish world, table fellowship was a declaration of social and spiritual solidarity. You ate with your equals, your community, your covenant partners. To share a meal with tax collectors and sinners was to declare them acceptable company — to treat the unclean as if they were clean. The Pharisees are not wrong about what Jesus is doing. They are wrong about what it means. The three parables that follow are Jesus' answer to their accusation, and each one reframes the question: the issue is not "why does Jesus eat with sinners?" but "what kind of God would not go looking for them?"

- **15:4-7 — The Parable of the Lost Sheep**: The opening phrase TIS ANTHRŌPOS EX HYMŌN ("which person among you") draws the Pharisees into the parable before they can resist it. Jesus forces them to imagine themselves as the shepherd. The detail that he leaves the ninety-nine EN TĒ ERĒMŌ ("in the wilderness" or "in the open country") is not incidental — leaving ninety-nine sheep unguarded in open terrain is risky. The shepherd's behavior borders on reckless, weighted entirely toward the one that is lost. When he finds it, he places it EPI TOUS ŌMOUS AUTOU ("on his shoulders") — this is the posture of a shepherd carrying a sheep that is too exhausted, injured, or frightened to walk. The image is one of extraordinary tenderness. The word SYNKALEI (synkalei, "calls together") in verse 6 indicates a gathering summoned for communal celebration — the joy is too great to be experienced alone. Verse 7 delivers the interpretive key: heaven itself rejoices more over one repenting sinner than over ninety-nine DIKAIOIS ("righteous ones") who have no need of repentance. The edge in this statement is directed squarely at the Pharisees, who consider themselves among the ninety-nine. Jesus does not dispute their righteousness — he simply tells them that the party in heaven is not about them.

- **15:8-10 — The Parable of the Lost Coin**: Jesus pairs a male protagonist (the shepherd) with a female one (the woman), a pattern characteristic of Luke's Gospel (see 2:25-38, 13:18-21). The coin is a DRACHMĒ (drachme), a Greek silver coin roughly equivalent to a day's wages for a laborer. Ten drachmas may represent the woman's entire savings, or they may be part of a headdress of coins worn as a dowry ornament — a common practice among Palestinian women, making the loss both financial and deeply personal. She lights a LYCHNON (lychnon, "lamp") because Palestinian peasant houses typically had small windows or none at all; the interior would be dark even during the day. She sweeps (SAROI, saroi) the earthen floor, listening for the clink of metal against stone. The deliberateness of the search — lamp, broom, careful attention — mirrors God's own intentional pursuit of the lost. Like the shepherd, the woman calls her community together to share her joy. The parable makes the same theological point as the first but from a different angle: God is not only the strong shepherd who ventures into the wilderness but also the careful woman who will not rest until what is lost is recovered.

- **15:11 — "A man had two sons"**: This is the most famous parable Jesus ever told, and its traditional title — "The Prodigal Son" — captures only one-third of the story. It is equally about the waiting father and the resentful older brother. The structure mirrors the chapter: the first two parables feature a lost sheep and a lost coin (things that cannot choose to be lost), while this parable features a lost son — someone who chose to leave. The escalation is deliberate. God seeks what is lost whether it wandered away by accident, fell into a dark corner, or walked out the door on purpose.

- **15:12 — "Father, give me my share of the estate"**: In Jewish law (Deuteronomy 21:17; Sirach 33:20-24), a father could distribute his estate before death, but the son would not normally receive full control of the assets until the father died. The younger of two sons would receive one-third (the elder receiving a double portion). What makes this request devastating is its subtext: the son is effectively saying, "I wish you were dead. Give me now what I would get when you die." In a culture where honoring one's father was the foundational commandment of social life, this request is an act of relational annihilation. The father's response — he simply divides the property (DIEILEN AUTOIS TON BION, "he divided his livelihood between them") — is the first of several shocks in this parable. There is no argument, no lecture, no punishment. He gives the son what he asks for. This is the portrait of a God who will not force anyone to stay.

- **15:13 — "squandered his wealth in reckless living"**: The word DIASKORPISEN (diaskorpisen) means "scattered" — the same word used for winnowing grain. He did not merely spend his inheritance; he flung it to the winds. The phrase ZŌNASŌTŌS (zōn asōtōs) means "living dissolutely" or "without saving" — the root ASŌTOS literally means "unsaveable" or "beyond recovery." Luke does not specify what the reckless living involved; that detail comes from the older brother's accusation in verse 30 (prostitutes), which may or may not be accurate. The older brother was not there; he may be inventing the worst possible version of events.

- **15:14-16 — "he began to be in need... he longed to fill his stomach with the pods the pigs were eating"**: The famine is the external crisis; the internal crisis is already complete. He has no money, no friends, no community. The phrase EKOLLĒTHĒ (ekollēthē, "he attached himself" or "he glued himself") to a citizen of that country suggests desperation — he clung to anyone who would have him. The job he is given is the ultimate degradation for a Jewish young man: feeding pigs. Pigs were unclean animals under the Torah (Leviticus 11:7); to tend them was to descend to the lowest rung of Gentile society. The KERATIA (keratia, "pods") are carob pods, sometimes called "locust beans" — animal feed. The devastating detail is OUDEIS EDIDOU AUTŌ ("no one gave him anything"). In the far country, there is no generosity, no community, no one who cares whether he lives or dies. This is the full cost of autonomy from the father: not freedom but utter isolation.

- **15:17-19 — "When he came to his senses"**: The phrase EIS HEAUTON DE ELTHŌN literally means "coming to himself" — it implies that his entire time away has been a kind of insanity, a departure not just from home but from his own true identity. The speech he rehearses is carefully constructed: he will confess sin against both heaven and his father (acknowledging the vertical and horizontal dimensions of his transgression), declare himself unworthy of sonship, and ask to be made a hired worker (MISTHIOS, misthios — a day laborer, the lowest tier of workers, below even household servants). This is not mere strategy; it is genuine recognition that he has forfeited every claim. What he does not yet understand is that his father's love is not a claim to be earned.

- **15:20 — "But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him"**: This is the emotional center of the entire chapter — arguably of the entire Gospel. The father SAW him while he was still ETI MAKRAN APECHONTOS ("still being far away"). This means the father was watching. He had been watching. Day after day, scanning the road, waiting for a figure on the horizon. The verb ESPLANCHNISTHĒ (esplanchnisthē, "was moved with compassion") comes from SPLANCHNA ("intestines, bowels") — it describes a visceral, gut-wrenching emotion, the deepest form of compassion in the Greek language. It is the same word used of Jesus when he sees the widow of Nain (7:13), the same word used of the Good Samaritan (10:33). Then — and this detail would have shocked every original listener — the father RUNS. DRAMŌN (dramōn, "having run"): in the ancient Near East, a dignified older man never ran. Running required hitching up one's robes, exposing the legs — it was considered shameful and undignified. This father abandons all dignity and sprints toward his broken son. He falls on his neck (EPEPESEN EPI TON TRACHĒLON AUTOU) and kisses him repeatedly (KATEPHILĒSEN — the prefix KATA- intensifies the verb, suggesting prolonged, passionate kissing). The son has not yet said a word. The father's response precedes any confession, any apology, any deal-making. This is grace in its purest form: love that runs toward the undeserving before they can even ask for it. Every theologian in history has recognized in this father the face of God.

- **15:21 — The son's speech**: He delivers the speech he rehearsed — but notice what is missing. He says, "I have sinned against heaven and against you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son." But he never gets to the third line: "Treat me as one of your hired workers." The father interrupts him. The son's demotion plan is never even heard, let alone considered. The father does not want a hired worker. He wants his son back.

- **15:22-24 — "Bring out the best robe... a ring... sandals"**: Each item the father commands is loaded with meaning. The STOLĒN TĒN PRŌTĒN ("the best robe" or "the first robe") — this is likely the father's own finest garment, a mark of honor and restored status. The DAKTYLION ("ring") for his finger is a signet ring, the symbol of family authority and the power to conduct business in the family's name. The HYPODĒMATA ("sandals") distinguish him from a slave — slaves went barefoot; sons wore shoes. In three swift commands, the father has completely reversed the son's descent. He arrived as a destitute pig-feeder; he is instantly restored as a son with full authority. The MOSCHON TON SITEUTON ("the fattened calf") was an animal kept specifically for a significant celebration — a wedding, a major festival. Killing it signals that this homecoming ranks among the greatest events in the family's history. The father's declaration — "this son of mine was NEKROS and is alive again, was APOLŌLOS and is found" — uses the language of resurrection. The return of the lost is not merely a happy ending; it is life from the dead.

- **15:25-28 — The older brother**: The parable now takes its sharpest turn. The older brother, who has been faithfully working EN AGRŌ ("in the field"), returns to find SYMPHŌNIAS KAI CHORŌN ("music and dancing") — a full-scale celebration he was not informed about. His reaction — ŌRGISTHĒ (ōrgisthē, "he became angry") and refusal to enter — mirrors the Pharisees' posture in verse 2 with surgical precision. He stands outside the party, resentful and self-righteous, just as the Pharisees stand outside the fellowship Jesus shares with sinners, grumbling. The father once again takes the initiative: EXELTHŌN ("having gone out"), he goes to his older son just as he ran to the younger one. The same father who ran down the road now walks out to the field. God pursues both the flagrant sinner and the self-righteous one. Neither son needs to come to the father first; the father comes to both.

- **15:29-30 — "All these years I have slaved for you"**: The older brother's language is revealing. He says EDOULEUON (edouleuon, "I have served as a slave") — not "I have worked alongside you" or "I have been your partner" but "I have slaved." He has been in the father's house his entire life but has experienced it as servitude rather than relationship. He has obeyed every command (ENTOLĒN, entolēn) but apparently without joy, without intimacy, without any sense that the father's home was his home too. He says "you never gave me even a young goat (ERIPHON) so I could celebrate with my friends" — a kid goat versus a fattened calf; his bitterness calculates the disparity with precision. And he refuses to say "my brother." Instead, he says HO HUIOS SOU HOUTOS — "this son of yours." He has disowned his brother in his speech just as surely as the younger son disowned the father by leaving. Both sons, in different ways, have rejected the family. This is one of Jesus' most devastating portraits of religious people: outwardly obedient but inwardly far from the father, resenting grace whenever it is extended to someone who has not earned it.

- **15:31-32 — "My child, you are always with me, and everything I have is yours"**: The father's response to the older son is breathtaking in its gentleness. He calls him TEKNON ("child" — a term of deep affection, not the formal HUIE, "son"). He does not argue, does not rebuke, does not defend the younger son's behavior. He simply states two truths: you are always with me, and everything I have is yours. The older son's complaint that he was never given a goat is answered by the revelation that he had access to everything all along — he simply never understood or claimed it. He was living as a slave in a house where he was a co-heir. Then the father reframes the celebration: EUPHRANTHENAI DE KAI CHARĒNAI EDEI — "it was necessary to celebrate and rejoice." The word EDEI ("it was necessary") carries the force of divine obligation. This is not optional generosity; it is the only possible response when the dead return to life. And the father now mirrors the older son's language back to him, corrected: not "this son of yours" but HO ADELPHOS SOU HOUTOS — "this brother of yours." The father will not let the older son disown his sibling. The parable ends without resolution. We never learn whether the older brother went in. That open ending is deliberate — it hangs in the air, directed at the Pharisees and scribes of verse 2, who must now decide: will they enter the celebration, or will they stand outside, furious that God's mercy extends to people they have written off? Luke leaves the door open. The father is still outside, still pleading. The choice remains.
